EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted northeast through the Western Atlantic and passed offshore of the Southeastern New England Shoreline. Snow accumulations on the order of 3 to 8 inches, with lighter amounts out on the Cape and Islands, were attributable with this storm. The light and fluffy nature of the snow combined with strong winds associated with the storm resulted in blowing and drifing, as well as visibility restrictions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across Tolland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough set up across New England...producing snow across much of Connecticut and western Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to nine inches of snow fell across Hartford County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pressed eastward through the region during the afternoon and evening hours on February 18th along and ahead of which a very mild and moist airmass existed. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front resulting in isolated sub-severe hail reports through Southeast New England. In wake of the cold front, strong winds gusted upwards of 60 mph causing minor damage with downed trees throughout the aforementioned region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported at 7:49 pm EST in Enfield, CT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge brought very hot temperatures to Southern New England. A moist southwest low level flow increased humidity levels such that heat index values rose above 105 degrees for a period of a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Hartford-Brainard Airport (KHFD) recorded heat indexes of 105 to 107 over an eight hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge brought very hot temperatures to Southern New England. A moist southwest low level flow increased humidity levels such that heat index values rose above 105 degrees for a period of a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Windham Airport (KIJD) near Willimantic recorded heat indexes of 105 to 108 over a seven hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering surface trough and an approaching cold front resulted in showers and thunderstorms developing across western Massachusetts. These thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs on Firetown Road were downed onto wires by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Southern New England, providing enough lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop. A large area of instability ahead of the front was enough for these storms to become severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.

A strong cold front moved across Southern New England, providing enough lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop. A large area of instability ahead of the front was enough for these storms to become severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches and wires on Taylor Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.  The Automated Surface Observing System at nearby Bradley International Airport (KBDL) recorded sustained wind speeds of 41 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility pole, wires, and tree limbs along Bailey Road and Parsonage Street were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessive weight from snow and ice caused numerous roof collapses across Southern Connecticut during the first week of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vacant Willard Road building in Norwalk experienced a partial roof collapse due to heavy snow and ice Thursday afternoon February 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifting through the eastern Mid-Atlantic and deepening through southeast New England into the Gulf of Maine resulted in an area of heavy rains with amounts ranging 2 to 5 inches across coastal and interior New England on top of melting snows. This large amount of water into various basins resulted in flooding of tributaries and major rivers, inundating local neighborhoods and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Willimantic River at Coventry went above flood cresting above the moderate flood stage of 11 feet.  In Ellington, Route 140 was closed near its intersection with Route 30 because of flooding.  Portions of Routes 6 and 66 in Columbia were also closed due to flooding.  Portions of Freshwater Boulevard in Enfield were flooded.  In Somers, portions of Durkee, Four Bridges, and King Roads adjacent to the Scantic River flooded.  Two cars got stuck on Flanders River Road in Coventry after it was closed due to flooding and had to be towed out by the fire department.  The drivers of both cars and a passenger were taken to Windham Community Memorial Hospital after showing signs of hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessive weight from snow and ice caused numerous roof collapses across Southern Connecticut during the second week of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weight of heavy snow and ice caused about 200 feet of a wall to collapse at the state armory building in New Haven, Monday Night February 7th.  The incident occurred around 6:30 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessive weight from snow and ice caused numerous roof collapses across Southern Connecticut during the second week of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 8:50 pm Monday night February 7th, the roof of Nutmeg Utility Products at 1755 Highland Avenue in Cheshire collapsed due to the weight of heavy snow and ice.  This forced the closing of Route 10 between West Johnson Avenue and School House Road until the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessive weight from snow and ice caused numerous roof collapses across Southern Connecticut during the first week of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn roof collapsed in Bethany at around 4 pm Wednesday afternoon February 2nd at the end of a cul-de-sac on Hunter Trail.  The weight of the snow and ice caused the roof to pancake and trap between 12 and 15 horses.  Rescue operations took 3 1/2 hours.  Also in Bethany, about 13 people escaped injury Wednesday afternoon when half of the roof collapsed at Fairfield County Millwork Inc., at 20 Sargent Drive. ||In Naugatuck, part of the roof at Thurston Energy at 410 Rubber Avenue, collapsed Thursday morning February 3rd under the weight of snow and ice.  The garage of a vacant house on the corner of Arch and Scott streets collapsed as well on Thursday. Mayor Robert A. Mezzo of Naugatuck declared a state of emergency Friday February 4th as a result of widespread structural damage to buildings across the borough due to heavy snow and ice loads.||In Wolcott, a garage at 1615 Meriden Road, used as storage for several aging school buses collapsed on Thursday around 1:30 pm.||In Meriden, the roof of a detached 3 car garage on 599 North Colony Street collapsed under the weight of snow and ice on Friday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessive weight from snow and ice caused numerous roof collapses across Southern Connecticut during the second week of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A real estate agency collapsed Tuesday morning, February 8th on Route 101 in Norwich due to the heavy weight of snow and ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elderly woman was found dead of exposure in Westport Connecticut on Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighty four year old Ada Eads was found dead of hypothermia or exposure to the cold weather on High Point Road in Westport Connecticut on Thursday morning around 8:00 am by trash collectors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system tracked northeast from the Lower Mississippi River Valley into the Ohio Valley Tuesday, February 1st into Tuesday Night , with a secondary low pressure developing over the Delmarva region and tracking southeast of Long Island on Wednesday February 2nd.  With a polar high locked in place across Ontario and Quebec, this slow moving storm system brought a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the region.  A bought of light snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred early Tuesday morning into Tuesday afternoon ahead of an approaching warm front, with a second heavier round of freezing rain and sleet for the region Tuesday Night into early Wednesday afternoon. This later episode caused numerous road closure and roof collapses across the region.||Generally 3 to 5 inches of snow and sleet fell across interior portions of Southern Connecticut during this 2 day storm, with 2 to 3 inches across southern portions. Between 1/4 and 3/4 of an inch of ice accreted across Southern Connecticut, with the highest amounts across far Southwestern Connecticut and interior Northeastern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tennis Club of Trumbull on Monroe Turnpike collapsed Tuesday evening beneath the mass of snow and ice on its sloped roof about 7:30 pm.

A slow moving frontal boundary gradually moved eastward across northwest Connecticut between Monday September 5th, and Tuesday September 6th, as waves of low pressure traveled northeast along the boundary. The front then moved south and east of the state by late Tuesday, as waves of low pressure continued to travel northeast along the boundary. In addition, copious amounts of moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee, which made landfall along the Gulf coast, interacted with the frontal system from Wednesday into Thursday September 8th, producing additional heavy rainfall. Total rainfall amounts across northwest Connecticut for the period from Monday into Thursday ranged from 3 to 6 inches. ||This heavy rainfall, combined with saturated soil from the excessive rains which fell in late August associated with the passage of Irene, led to widespread minor to moderate flooding on rivers, as well as small streams and creeks across northwest Connecticut. Some major flooding did occur across portions of the Housatonic River. Flash flooding also occurred in the vicinity of Woodbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Woodbury Center Route 317, Route 47 and Judson Avenue were closed due to flooding. Major flooding occurred on the Pomperaug River at Southbury New Haven County. The river gage located near Poverty Road in Southbury exceeded its 9 foot flood stage around 6:45 am EST, its 10 foot moderate flood stage around 7:45 am, its 12 foot major flood stage around 8:45 am, it crested at 15.64 feet around 11 am and fell below flood stage by 5:30 pm September 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure centers impacted the Southern New England Region with a combination of heavy snows and ice.  The first area of low pressure on February 1st lifted northeastward offshore of the Southeastern New England shoreline ushering heavy snows across the interior portions of New England, especially north and west.  A second area of low pressure deepened through the Ohio River Valley, redeveloping over the Southeastern New England shoreline bringing a combination of heavy snows, sleet and freezing rain over much of the region February 2nd, before changing back to all snow into the end of the event.||A series of significant heavy snow events occurred between December 26, 2010 and February 2, 2011.  Snow for the winter season totaled 86.4 inches, most of which fell during this period.  Across Connecticut, numerous roof collapses due to heavy snow load occurred following the February 2nd storm, including 75 structures in Hartford County, 28 structures in Windham County, and 31 structures in Tolland County.  Damage amounts for this storm include these roof collapses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 6-11 inches of snow fell across Hartford County over the two day period, with upwards of a quarter inch of ice accumulation for isolated locations falling during the morning perod on the 2nd.  Damage amounts are for the roof collapses of some 75 structures that occurred following heavy snowfall that totaled 86.4 inches by the end of the snow season.  Most of this snow fell from December 26 through February 2 and most roof collapses occurred during or shortly after the February 1 and 2 snow storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic and unprecedented early-season winter storm impacted the area on Saturday, October 29, with more than one foot of heavy wet snow falling on interior portions of Southern Connecticut.  This is the first time a winter storm of this magnitude has ever occurred in October.||Late Friday night, October 28, an area of low pressure emerged from the South Carolina coastal plain into the western Atlantic Ocean.  The low moved up the coast late Friday night into Saturday morning.  The low began to rapidly strengthen late Saturday morning when it was east of the Delmarva coast, and continued to strengthen as it passed south of Long Island Saturday night.  With water temperatures in the lower 60s, extreme southeast coastal Connecticut received mainly rainfall and up to 3 inches of rain during the event.  Areas just to the north and west, however, were just cold enough to support a heavy wet snow.  The heaviest snow fell along a corridor through interior southwest Connecticut, with up to a foot of snow in the highest elevations.  In addition to the heavy rain and snow, strong winds were experienced along the immediate coastline. ||Hundreds of thousands of people across southern Connecticut lost power during this event as heavy snow accumulated on trees that still had partial to full foliage during mid-autumn.  This caused extensive felling of trees and limbs across the region and damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage and power outages occurred due to 3 to 8 inches of heavy wet snow and 15 to 20 mph winds. ||In Stamford, trees and branches and power lines were reported down throughout the town around 1230 pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front triggered isolated severe thunderstorms in New London County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down at the intersection of Harland and Cuprack Roads in the town of Norwich.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front triggered isolated severe thunderstorms in New London County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on power lines on Lantern Hill Road in Stonington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of showers and thunderstorms in association with a slow moving cold front and deep tropical moisture moving up the east coast from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee, produced a prolonged period of rainfall, which led to river flooding in Fairfield and New Haven Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Southbury, two feet of water infiltrated the first floor of 15 homes along the Pomperaug River.  The river gage on the Pomperaug River at Southbury exceeded its flood stage of 9.0 ft. on 9/8/2011 at 7:45am EDT, crested at 14.78 ft. at 1:15pm EDT and fell back within its banks at 6:30 pm EDT.  River Rd., River Trail and East Flat Hill Rd. also in Southbury, were closed due to following. Total rainfall amounts across New Haven County ranged from 3.04 in Oxford to 5.60 in Wallingford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system originating from the deep south brought heavy snow and sleet to Litchfield County Connecticut. Initially light snow overspread the area as a result of a weak area of low pressure moving northeastward off the mid Atlantic and northeast coasts on Tuesday, February 1st. A much stronger low approached from the Ohio Valley Tuesday night and crossed the region on Wednesday, February 2nd. ||Snowfall reports across Litchfield County ranged from 8 to 11 inches across the northern portion of the county, with 5 to 7 inches across the southern portion.

A low pressure system approached from the Great Lakes region Friday night, January 7th, while a secondary area of low pressure formed along the mid Atlantic coast. These lows moved slowly eastward Friday night into Saturday, eventually consolidating into one low during the day Saturday, January 8th. Four to 10 inches of snow fell across Litchfield County.

Bitterly cold air settled into the region as Canadian high pressure built in Sunday night January 23rd, into early Monday morning on January 24th. Temperatures plummeted to 5 to 15 degrees below zero across Litchfield County. Brisk westerly winds diminished during the evening, becoming light and variable to calm after midnight. The winds resulted in wind chill readings of 15 to 25 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along the southeast coast Tuesday, January 11th, and explosive deepening, cyclogenesis, occurred Tuesday night as the low tracked northeastward. The combination of showers and thunderstorms in association with a slow moving cold front and deep tropical moisture moving up the east coast from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee, produced a prolonged period of rainfall, which led to river flooding in Fairfield and New Haven Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pembroke Elementary School in Danbury was closed for the day due to flooding in the building.  The back parking lot at Danbury Police Headquarters on Main St. was completely flooded with several cars submerged in water up to the bottom of the windows.  In Ridgefield, a portion of George Washington Hwy., as well as Shadow Lake Rd. were closed due to flooding. Total rainfall amounts across Fairfield County ranged from 2.99 inches in Success Hill to 6.39 inches in Danbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the east coast bringing heavy rain and strong to damaging winds to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a wind gust of 61 mph.  A tree on English Neighborhood Road in Woodstock was downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic and unprecedented early-season winter storm impacted the area on Saturday, October 29, with more than one foot of heavy wet snow falling on interior portions of Southern Connecticut.  This is the first time a winter storm of this magnitude has ever occurred in October.||Late Friday night, October 28, an area of low pressure emerged from the South Carolina coastal plain into the western Atlantic Ocean.  The low moved up the coast late Friday night into Saturday morning.  The low began to rapidly strengthen late Saturday morning when it was east of the Delmarva coast, and continued to strengthen as it passed south of Long Island Saturday night.  With water temperatures in the lower 60s, extreme southeast coastal Connecticut received mainly rainfall and up to 3 inches of rain during the event.  Areas just to the north and west, however, were just cold enough to support a heavy wet snow.  The heaviest snow fell along a corridor through interior southwest Connecticut, with up to a foot of snow in the highest elevations.  In addition to the heavy rain and snow, strong winds were experienced along the immediate coastline. ||Hundreds of thousands of people across southern Connecticut lost power during this event as heavy snow accumulated on trees that still had partial to full foliage during mid-autumn.  This caused extensive felling of trees and limbs across the region and damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage and power outages occurred due to 2 to 4 inches of heavy wet snow and 15 to 20 mph winds.||In New Haven, multiple trees were reported down at Pine and Ferry Streets around 430 pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong upper level disturbance and a slow moving inverted surface trough extending back over the region from offshore low pressure brought an unusual snow event to the region on Friday, January seventh. Light snow developed across southwest Connecticut during the morning, with a narrow band of heavy snow developing by early Friday afternoon. This band of heavy snow, with snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour, then slowly translated east across Southern Connecticut  Friday afternoon through Friday evening. Snow totals ranged from 6 to 15 inches across northwestern portions of Southern Connecticut, with 4 to 8 inches across the remainder of this region.

Southern Connecticut received a heavy snowfall on the 11th and 12th of January from surface low pressure tracking up the Atlantic seaboard and then rapidly intensifying as it tracked just southeast of Long Island in response to an intense upper level disturbance. ||Light snow overspread the region Tuesday evening ahead of low pressure and then became heavier Tuesday night as low pressure rapidly intensified. As the upper level disturbance worked over the region late Tuesday night a semi-stationary north to south band of very heavy snow developed just east of the Hudson River, gradually pivoting northeast Wednesday morning through early Wednesday afternoon. This band was responsible for snowfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour, and snow totals of between 15 and 30 inches across Southern CT by Wednesday afternoon.||The highest snowfall totals were observed across northern portions of Fairfield and New Haven Counties. The Governor of CT, Daniel P. Malloy, was cited as saying, ���that the season's second major storm was a record-breaker, dumping more snow on Connecticut in 12 hours than the state had ever seen in a full day, up to 30 inches.

Weak low pressure tracked up the coast Tuesday and then just southeast of Long Island Tuesday night, bringing a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the region. With cold air in place initially, light snow overspread the region Tuesday evening. Then as warmer air began to work into the region aloft late Tuesday night into early Wednesday morning, the precipitation changed to sleet and eventually freezing rain, before ending as a mix of freezing rain and rain Wednesday afternoon. Between 2 and 3 inches of snow and sleet fell across the region Tuesday night, with one quarter to one half of an inch of ice accumulation on top of that. This made for dangerous road conditions for the Wednesday morning commute.

Southern Connecticut received a heavy snowfall on the 11th and 12th of January from surface low pressure tracking up the Atlantic seaboard and then rapidly intensifying as it tracked just southeast of Long Island in response to an intense upper level disturbance. ||Light snow overspread the region Tuesday evening ahead of low pressure and then became heavier Tuesday night as low pressure rapidly intensified. As the upper level disturbance worked over the region late Tuesday night a semi-stationary north to south band of very heavy snow developed just east of the Hudson River, gradually pivoting northeast Wednesday morning through early Wednesday afternoon. This band was responsible for snowfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour, and snow totals of between 15 and 30 inches across Southern CT by Wednesday afternoon.||The highest snowfall totals were observed across northern portions of Fairfield and New Haven Counties. The Governor of CT, Daniel P. Malloy, was cited as saying, ���that the season's second major storm was a record-breaker, dumping more snow on Connecticut in 12 hours than the state had ever seen in a full day, up to 30 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A partial roof collapse occurred at a multi-unit apartment building at 92 Washington Street in Norwich, CT at 10:53 am on Wednesday. No one was injured, but 10 people were displaced. The collapse was due to roof rafters on the nearly flat roof giving out under the weight of heavy snow and ice. The roof partially collapsed, causing the rear wall to bulge and for the building's chimney to lean towards an adjacent building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of moderate to heavy snow overspread the region Wednesday morning into Wednesday afternoon on January 26th in response to a subtropical moisture feed ahead of a Mid Atlantic low pressure system. This bout of snow produced 2 to 5 inches across the region. The snow tapered to a light wintry mix late Wednesday afternoon into early evening, before a second heavier round of precipitation with convection worked into the region Wednesday evening. This was in response to a strong upper level disturbance working towards the region and rapidly intensifying coastal low pressure southeast of the region throwing moisture back over the area. A very heavy snow band developed over Southwest Connecticut Wednesday evening, that gradually moved northeast late Wednesday night. This band was responsible for snowfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour over a 4 to 6 hr period that raised snow totals to 12-20 inches across most of the region.

Weak low pressure tracked up the coast Tuesday and then just southeast of Long Island Tuesday night, bringing a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the region. With cold air in place initially, light snow overspread the region Tuesday evening. Then as warmer air began to work into the region aloft late Tuesday night into early Wednesday morning, the precipitation changed to sleet and eventually freezing rain, before ending as a mix of freezing rain and rain Wednesday afternoon. Between 2 and 3 inches of snow and sleet fell across the region Tuesday night, with one quarter to one half of an inch of ice accumulation on top of that. This made for dangerous road conditions for the Wednesday morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 3000 customers lost power Tuesday night on January 18 and a section of Route 10 was shut down for about 2 hours in Cheshire due to snow and ice. About 26 percent of Cheshire was without power.

A heavy snowpack after multiple snowstorms since the end of December caused multiple roof collapse events across Southern Connecticut during the month of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a vacant building at 1198 New Haven Road in Naugatuck collapsed under the weight of heavy accumulated snow.

A developing nor'easter coastal storm dumped up to two and a half feet of snow across Connecticut in a 24 hour period.  Twenty-two and a half inches fell at Bradley International Airport setting a one day snowfall record for that location.||This was the second major storm of an above average winter of snowfall.  The first occurred December 26 and 27, with several other relatively minor snowfalls in the month of January, and a third major storm February 1 and 2.  With only a brief thaw in between the December storm and the January storm, snow piled up across southern New England resulting in numerous roof collapses, towns seeking permission to dump excess snow in area rivers and bays, and numerous disruptions to transportation.  Federal assistance was sought by Governor Malloy for costs associated with the January 12 winter storm and its cleanup.  It was granted by President Obama for Hartford and Tolland counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seventeen to twenty-nine inches of snow fell across Tolland County.

Another barn collapsed around 8:30 am that morning at the intersection of Currituck Road and Hedge Meadow Lane, while a third barn collapsed around the same time on the intersection of Currituck Road and Butterfield Road.

A vertically stacked upper level low moved slowly over eastern Pennsylvania, New Jersey and southern New York on Friday, May 20th. The surface low moved to near Long Island by the early afternoon. Showers and thunderstorms developed in a moist and unstable airmass across the region. Storms ���trained��� or repeatedly moved over the same areas across a portion of Litchfield County resulting in flash flooding in the Waterbury area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported northwest of Waterbury in Litchfield County at the intersection of Route 6 and Route 63, on Route 63 between Echo Lake Road and Woodruff Avenue and on portions of Woodruff Avenue and Scott Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary low pressure system over the Mid-Atlantic States produced heavy showers and thunderstorms that resulted in isolated flooding across part of New Haven County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous cars were under water at the on ramp of Interstates 91 and 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of low pressure tracked north along a slow moving cold front that extended from the Gulf Coast to Maine.  The combination of this slow moving boundary and a sub-tropical moisture feed aided in the production of heavy rainfall that resulted in moderate to major flooding across portions of Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Branch of the Saugatuck River exceeded bankfull causing basement flooding in the town of Westport.  Easton Road, Lyons Plains Road, and Highway 136 were closed and evacuations were performed on Saugatuck Ave. due to flooding of the Saugatuck River, also in Westport.  The intersection of Federal Rd. and Rt. 7 in Brookfield was flooded with approximately two to four inches of standing water forcing car dealerships to evacuate.  The Still River exceeded bankfull causing Rt. 6 and Newtown Rd. to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of low pressure tracked north along a slow moving cold front that extended from the Gulf Coast to Maine.  The combination of this slow moving boundary and a sub-tropical moisture feed aided in the production of heavy rainfall that resulted in moderate to major flooding across portions of Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three vehicles and two structures were seen floating down the Housatonic River in Oxford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure continued to deepen across the Southeastern Canadian Maritimes as high pressure pressed eastward through the Central Plains and the Great Lakes Region into the Ohio River Valley. The interaction of the two systems resulted in a tight pressure gradient across New England to continue with strong north-northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator in Tolland, CT recorded a wind gust of 59 mph on their home weather station at 11:13 am EST.  In addition, a large tree and utility pole were downed in Vernon, CT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure continued to deepen across the Southeastern Canadian Maritimes as high pressure pressed eastward through the Central Plains and the Great Lakes Region into the Ohio River Valley. The interaction of the two systems resulted in a tight pressure gradient across New England to continue with strong north-northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Bradley International Airport (KBDL) recorded a sustained wind of 44 mph at 1:33 pm EST.  In addition, multiple trees were downed across Hartford County, including one that was downed onto a garage on Halwood Drive in Granby, another on a garage in East Hartford, and two on April Drive and Indian Hill Road in Glastonbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved gradually southeastward across the region during the day Monday, March 7th, as a wave of low pressure moved northeastward along the boundary. To the south of the boundary, it was mild as the area was in the warm sector of the low pressure system. The storm tapped into both Atlantic and Gulf moisture, resulting in heavy rainfall of 3 to 4 inches across Litchfield County Sunday, March 6th, into Monday, March 7th. The heavy rainfall, combined with runoff from snowmelt due to the mild temperatures, resulted in widespread flooding of rivers, streams, creeks, roads, and basements. Evacuations occurred in some communities as flood waters cut residences off from main roads.||Damage estimates are unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sections of Route 7 were closed due to flooding from heavy rainfall and from the Housatonic River overflowing its banks from Kent to just south of the Veterans Bridge, Route 202, in New Milford. Widespread street and basement flooding was reported New Milford. A mudslide occurred on Grove Street in New Milford which prompted the evacuation of several families on Monday, March 7th. It was reported about 40 families were evacuated in Kent due to flood waters.  ||Moderate flooding occurred along the Housatonic River through Litchfield County. The river gauge at Falls Village crested at 11.6 feet, 4.6 feet above flood stage and the gauge at Gaylordsville crested at 13.8 feet, 5.8 feet above flood stage. Farther downstream the river gauge at Stevenson Dam experienced major flooding with a crest of 21.7 feet, 10.7 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifting through the eastern Mid-Atlantic and deepening through southeast New England into the Gulf of Maine resulted in an area of heavy rains with amounts ranging 2 to 5 inches across coastal and interior New England on top of melting snows. This large amount of water into various basins resulted in flooding of tributaries and major rivers, inundating local neighborhoods and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mainstem rivers and small streams in Hartford County went into flood.  These include the Farmington River at Tariffville, Unionville, and Simsbury, the Bunnell Brook at Burlington, and the Quinnipiac River at Southington.  In addition, basements were flooded in Berlin and Avon.  Several intersections were flooded, including the intersection of Old Farms and Tillotson Roads in Avon and the intersection of Wolcott and Wescott Roads in Simsbury.  In New Britain, sewers backed up on Pierremount Avenue, flooding numerous houses.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mainstem rivers and small streams in Hartford County went into flood.  These include, the Hockanum River at East Hartford and the Connecticut River at Hartford.  Several basements flooded in Windsor and Windsor Locks, including one on Gaylord Road in Windsor Locks.  Bloomfield Avenue was closed near the Bloomfield/Windsor line because a nearby pond overflowed its banks.

An area of low pressure developed along the mid Atlantic coast during the day Wednesday, January 26th. The low strengthened as it moved northeastward, passing just south and east of Cape Cod early Thursday morning, January 27th. Seven to nearly 11 inches of snow accumulated across southern Litchfield County, with lesser amounts of 5 to 7 inches across the northern portion of the county.

Irene made landfall locally as a tropical storm around 900 am EDT on August 28, 2011 over New York City.  Irene then moved across Southeast New York and Western Connecticut before dissipating over Northern New England near the Canadian Border later that evening. Copious amounts of tropical moisture within the storm produced extended periods of heavy rainfall, which resulted in widespread moderate to major flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sections of White St. were closed with several feet of water on it in Danbury.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Naugatuck River overflowed its banks resulting in flooding in downtown Seymour.  Water breached the bridge on Rt. 67 and evacuations were performed on Derby Ave. in Derby.  Center St. in Shelton was also flooded. The Naugatuck River at Beacon Falls and Waterbury exceeded their respective flood stages of 12.0 ft and 12.8 ft and crested at 14.36 ft. and 15.77 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved across Southern New England and stalled just south of the area.  This front was instrumental in bringing tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee into New England.  A series of shortwaves moved through the northeast during this time period bringing several periods of showers and steady rainfall to parts of Southern New England.  Rainfall totals throughout the region over the four days totaled anywhere from two to eight inches, with most areas receiving four to six inches.  This resulted in flooding both on the rivers and small streams and in urban areas.  The bulk of the flooding in urban areas occurred on Sept. 8 as a band of very heavy rain moved through, dumping up to two inches of rain in an hour to hour and a half in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed throughout Bloomfield because of water one to two feet deep over the roads.  In addition, a ten foot section of Bloomfield Avenue was washed out near the intersection of Bloomfield Avenue and Route 218.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an intensifying low pressure system moved northeast, its cold front swept east across the region and produced high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 61 mph was measured at Groton New London Airport at 10:21 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an intensifying low pressure system moved northeast, its cold front swept east across the region and produced high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 63 mph was measured at New Haven Airport at 9:25 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing cyclonic flow aloft, a shortwave rotating through the upper level trough and a cold front moving across the Southern New England region created enough instability and lift to produce isolated thunderstorms across northern Connecticut. One of these storms produced severe thunderstorm winds that resulted in tree damage in Hartford, Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree on Pliny Street was downed by thunderstorm winds, landing on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant severe weather event occurred across east central New York and adjacent western New England, including Litchfield County on Thursday June 9th. A very warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on the morning of June 9th. A cold front was positioned well to the north and west of the region, toward the Upper Great Lakes. However, a well-defined pre-frontal trough marched across Litchfield County late in the afternoon. This pre-frontal trough showed up well in observational data, with an abrupt wind shift to the northwest and a sharp drop in dew points. The pre-frontal trough triggered convection, first as a series of discrete storms developing into a broken line, which eventually evolved into an organized line of severe storms known as a severe quasi-linear-convective-system, QLCS. Initially when the storms were discrete, hail was the main threat. Once the QLCS developed, damaging winds were the main threat. The severe storms resulted in numerous power outages with the large hail denting vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in Canaan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Southern New England, providing enough lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop. A large area of instability ahead of the front was enough for these storms to become severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires in the Pleasant Street area were downed by thunderstorm winds.  A few of these trees fell on houses.  Trees and branches also were reported down on Route 6 and Riverside Drive.  A rubber membrane roof was blown off an apartment house landing on Bridge Street.  A tree fell onto a minivan on Windham Center Road.  No one was injured, but one passenger was taken to the hospital for evaluation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across the region during Wednesday afternoon and evening, June 1st. Atmospheric conditions were favorable for severe thunderstorms including supercells with an explosion environment in place across the region. The thunderstorms across the region during the afternoon hours had strong and persistent (sometimes rotating) updrafts. ||Severe thunderstorms occurred in Litchfield County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous branches, 6-12 inches in diameter, were reported down which took out some power lines on West Hill Road in Barkhamsted due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Irene formed east of the Caribbean island of Dominica, part of the Lesser Antilles region, on the afternoon of August 20. Irene moved through the Caribbean and up the east coast of the United States making landfall twice. She first made landfall as a Category 1 Hurricane near Cape Lookout, North Carolina around 7:30am on August 27, then moved offshore again during the evening. She then made a 2nd landfall, again as a Category 1 Hurricane at 540am on August 28 near Little Egg Inlet in New Jersey. She moved over New York City and then into southeastern New York State and Connecticut as a Tropical Storm a few hours later. By the end of the evening of the 28th, Irene was crossing the U.S./Canada border having produced significant amounts of rain, storm surge, inland and coastal flooding, and wind damage across southern New England and much of the east coast of the United States. ||In Southern New England, the minimum surface pressure recorded was 976.9mb taken at Barnes Municipal Airport in Westfield, Massachusetts. The storm surge experienced along the coast was generally in the two to four foot range with a high of 4.78 feet at Fox Point in Providence, Rhode Island. The highest sustained windspeed was 54 knots (62 mph) at the Physical Oceanographic Real Time System station at Conimicut Light in Narragansett Bay, RI. The highest sustained wind speed on land was 38 knots (44 mph) recorded on the Automated Surface Observing Systems at both Barnstable Municipal Airport in Hyannis, MA (KHYA) and Logan International Airport in Boston, MA (KBOS). Rainfall amounts ranged from nearly zero (0.03 at Nantucket Memorial Airport - ACK) to nearly 10 inches (9.92 in Conway, MA). ||Despite the relatively low wind speeds, sustained winds over a 6 to 12 hour long duration resulted in widespread tree damage and resulted in power outages to roughly half a million customers throughout the state. Some of these customers did not get their power back until the Friday following the storm (some five days later). Durring the passage of Tropical Storm Irene, the winds resulted in $60M in property damages and inland flooding resulted in 1 fatality and $8M in property damages. ||Bunnell Brook in Burlington had its third worst flood on record.  The Farmington River reached its highest levels since Tropical Storms Connie and Diane in 1955.||The collective effects of Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, resulted in 1 fatality, 0 injuries, and $127.3M in property damage in the following counties: Barnstable, Bristol, Essex, Franklin, Hampden, Hampshire, Middlesex, Nantucket, Norfolk, Plymouth, Suffolk, and Worcester (all in MA), Hartford, Tolland, and Windham (all in CT), Cheshire and Hillsborough (all in NH), and Bristol, Providence, Kent, Washington, and Newport (all in RI).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and branches were downed in Moosup (Lake Street) and Plainfield (Route 12, Major and Gendron Roads, Huntington Estates).  An amateur radio operator recorded gusts to 57 knots (66 mph) on their home weather station in Thompson.  The Automated Surface Observing System at Windham Airport (KIJD) recorded sustained wind speeds of 24 knots (28 mph) and gusts to 44 knots (51 mph).

Several of these trees fell onto houses.  The Automated Surface Observing System at Bradley International Airport (KBDL) recorded sustained wind speeds of 26 knots (30 mph) and wind gusts to 44 knots (51 mph).  The Automated Surface Observing System at Hartford-Brainard Airport (KHFD) recorded sustained winds of 21 knots (24 mph) and wind gusts to 40 knots (46 mph).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to ten inches of heavy rain from Tropical Storm Irene resulted in the Pequabuck River overflowing its banks.  This led to flooding on roads and homes along the river throughout Bristol.  Flooded roads ran approximately three and a half feet deep.  A 46 year old man went canoeing in floodwaters.  He drowned when his canoe capsized and he was unable to make it to shore.  At least 50 people were evacuated from areas around the river, and took shelter at Chippens Hill Middle School.  The river flooding undermined at least 7 feet of Route 72 eastbound, washing out this portion of the road.  Numerous houses and businesses were affected by the flooding.  Forty high water rescues took place in Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough and an approaching cold front triggered scattered severe thunderstorms across Southern Connecticut producing damaging wind gusts across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across Birch Heights Road in Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary gradually moved eastward across northwest Connecticut between Monday September 5th, and Tuesday September 6th, as waves of low pressure traveled northeast along the boundary. The front then moved south and east of the state by late Tuesday, as waves of low pressure continued to travel northeast along the boundary. In addition, copious amounts of moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee, which made landfall along the Gulf coast, interacted with the frontal system from Wednesday into Thursday September 8th, producing additional heavy rainfall. Total rainfall amounts across northwest Connecticut for the period from Monday into Thursday ranged from 3 to 6 inches. ||This heavy rainfall, combined with saturated soil from the excessive rains which fell in late August associated with the passage of Irene, led to widespread minor to moderate flooding on rivers, as well as small streams and creeks across northwest Connecticut. Some major flooding did occur across portions of the Housatonic River. Flash flooding also occurred in the vicinity of Woodbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Housatonic River in Licthfield County. The Falls Village river gage located near Water Street in Falls Village exceeded its 7 foot flood stage at 5 pm EST September 6th, its 10 foot moderate flood stage at 11:49 pm September 8th, it crested at 13.47 feet at 6 am September 9th, then remained above its moderate flood stage until 7:15 am September 11th, and finally fell below flood stage at 2:34 pm September 12th. The Gaylordsville river gage located near Route 7, Kent Road, less than a mile north of the Village of Gaylordsville exceeded its 8 foot flood stage at 6 pm EST September 6th, its 10 foot moderate flood stage at 5 pm September 7th, then remained above its moderate flood stage until 9 am September 11th, and finally fell below flood stage at 3 pm September 12th. The crest time and level are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season Nor'easter dumped heavy wet snow on Litchfield County. The storm tapped both Atlantic and subtropical moisture. Snowfall rates were as high as 2 to 4 inches an hour in mesoscale snowbands. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 1 1/2 feet across the county. Power outages occurred as trees and wires came down due to the heavy snow. The outages were the most widespread and prolonged in areas where leaves were still on the trees. ||A colder air mass was brought into the region on Thursday, October 27th, as an area of low pressure moved eastward along a frontal boundary to our south. This weak system produced our first snowfall of season. Low pressure began to develop along the southeast coast Friday night. The low tracked along or just north of the Gulf Stream and rapidly deepened and intensified (cyclogenesis) as it moved northeastward on Saturday, October 29th. The storm passed just southeast of Cape Cod early Sunday morning, October 30th, then headed northeastward into the Canadian Maritimes during the day.

As Hurricane Irene moved north along the Atlantic coast and interacted with land, it weakened and made its second landfall as a Tropical Storm near Little Egg Inlet along the southeast NJ Coast on August 28, 2011 around 5:35 am EDT.  Tropical Storm Irene made its third landfall in New York City around 9:00 am.||Irene brought sustained tropical storm winds, heavy rain, and destructive storm surge.||One death and two injuries resulted from high winds:||1 woman was struck by a fallen power line in Prospect (New Haven County)||2 fire fighters were ���shocked��� by fallen power lines as they tried to rescue the woman in Prospect||Approximately 15,000 people evacuated from storm surge along the CT shores of Long Island Sound where a state voluntary evacuation was in effect.  ||Preliminary damage cost estimates included $150-200 million for Individual Assistance covered by Insurance and $40-50 million for Public Assistance.||The number of power outages state wide peaked around 900,000 outages.  The state lost around 3 percent of their tree population.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The maximum sustained winds meaasured by the ASOS at Sikorsky Airport in Bridgeport were east at 40 knots.  The peak gust was northeast at 55 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing strong cold front and associated upper level trough triggered numerous severe thunderstorms across Southwest Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down alongside Route 39 in Sherman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare and historic October Nor'easter brought very heavy snow to portions of southern New England on Saturday October 29.  Low pressure tracked northeast from the North Carolina coast Saturday morning, rapidly strengthening as it passed well south of Nantucket Saturday evening. As the storm intensified, colder air from aloft was drawn into New England resulting in heavy snow in the interior. ||Snowfall accumulations of one to two feet were common in the Monadnocks, Berkshires, Connecticut Valley, and higher elevations in central Massachusetts. Up to 31 inches of snow was reported at Jaffrey, New Hampshire and Plainfield, Massachusetts. Minor accumulations were even reported down to the south coast as the rain changed to a period of snow late Saturday night before ending. The accumulation of the heavy wet snow on trees and power lines resulted in widespread tree damage and power outages across many communities in central and western Massachusetts, southern New Hampshire, and northeastern Connecticut.  This resulted in school closures and numerous towns cancelled or rescheduled Halloween and trick-or-treating activities. At the peak, 830,000 customers in Connecticut were without power.  Throughout Connecticut, 164 AT&T cell phone towers were damaged resulting in degraded cell phone service until towers could be repaired and power restored.||Air travel in and around the Hartford area was disrupted when numerous flights were diverted to Bradley International Airport from the New York City metro area and then power outages affected the airport.  Several airplanes were not able to allow their passengers to disembark for seven hours or more.  ||A motorist in Hebron died in a car accident believed to be the result of the weather conditions.||The precipitation started as mainly snow early Saturday afternoon across the interior of southern New England, although a brief period of rain at the onset was common across the lower elevations. Several hours of heavy snow occurred from mid afternoon through late evening on Saturday October 29. Snowfall rates reached 3 inches per hour for several hours in the Berkshires and Monadnocks as a nearly stationary band of heavy snow set up across this region. The snow tapered off just after midnight Saturday night in western New England with the last of the precipitation exiting eastern New England Sunday morning. This storm also brought damaging winds to Cape Cod and the islands with wind gusts up to 70 mph occurring early Sunday morning October 30 as well as minor to moderate coastal flooding to east coastal Massachusetts during the high tide early Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seventeen inches of snow fell across Hartford County.  Heavy wet snow fell on foliated trees, breaking branches and downing trees and wires, resulting in widespread power outages that lasted for up to 11 days. The Enfield DMV roof was compromised by the heavy snow and was shutdown. Both Avon and South Windsor have estimated 100,000 cubic yards of debris from fallen trees and power lines. The estimated cleanup costs across several towns in Hartford County is between $1.5 to $2.5 million. In addition, the Glastonbury Pheasant Farm lost more than 4,000 birds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant severe weather event occurred across east central New York and adjacent western New England, including Litchfield County on Thursday June 9th. A very warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on the morning of June 9th. A cold front was positioned well to the north and west of the region, toward the Upper Great Lakes. However, a well-defined pre-frontal trough marched across Litchfield County late in the afternoon. This pre-frontal trough showed up well in observational data, with an abrupt wind shift to the northwest and a sharp drop in dew points. The pre-frontal trough triggered convection, first as a series of discrete storms developing into a broken line, which eventually evolved into an organized line of severe storms known as a severe quasi-linear-convective-system, QLCS. Initially when the storms were discrete, hail was the main threat. Once the QLCS developed, damaging winds were the main threat. The severe storms resulted in numerous power outages with the large hail denting vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and wires were reported down on Route 7 in Kent between Bulls Bridge Road and Route 341 due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching upper level disturbance and a stationary front in the vicinity produced thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall that caused flash flooding in Middlesex and New Haven Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused a number of basements of government buildings and individual homes and businesses to be flooded throughout the town of Portland.  The flooding caused extensive damage to town and school records and other materials that were stored in the basement of the Brownstone Intermediate School on Main St. and the Senior Center on Waverly Ave., which houses the Portland Food Bank.  Water rescues were also performed by local authorities on Main St. near the Arrigoni Bridge which stretches over the Connecticut River. Total property damage is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front and mid level shortwave combined to produce severe thunderstorms across Southwest Connecticut, with several reports of large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and numerous wires were reported down throughout Ansonia, including wires  reported down on a house along Wakelee Avenue, near Hull Street, and a large tree along Farther Salemi Drive, and a tree on a car on Platt Street. The roof of the Ansonia Armory was also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching upper level disturbance and a stationary front in the vicinity produced thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall that caused flash flooding in Middlesex and New Haven Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Seymour, several motorists were rescued from their vehicles on the Rt. 8 bridge between exits 22 and 23. Firefighters also rescued motorists in the Pearl St. area.  The bridge on North St. between Smith St. and Beach St. was closed after becoming compromised by raging waters in the Bladens River below.  Many basements were flooded throughout town.  Several road washouts occurred including Rt. 67 between East St. and Park Rd. and on portions of Pearl, Beach and School Streets after flood waters as deep as two ft. swept over the streets. A retaining wall in front of Walgreens on Rt. 67, between Martha St. and Old Dr., partially collapsed from the flood waters. The Seymour Land Trust property, a cherished passive recreation complex, on Chatfield St. is estimated to have suffered over $750,000 in damage when a flood of water rushed through the property and washed out bridges and trails. Paved paths buckled and crumbled due to the tremendous amount of water that rushed in. A 20-by-15 crater some 30-ft. deep was carved on the grounds.   ||West Rd. in Beacon Falls was also partially washed away due to the excessive rain.||Local media reported a total of 4.25 inches of rainfall in Seymour.  Total property damage is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure passed to the south of the Tri-State Area. High precipitable water values caused very heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding in portions of Fairfield and New Haven counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Milford, Route 1, also known as Boston Post Rd., was closed due to flooding.  Water filled the lower level of the Westfield Connecticut Post Mall, also on Rt. 1, forcing the closure of lower level businesses.  Water damage forced the closing of the Milford Senior Center.  The Milford Fire Dept. responded to multiple calls of flooding and impassible roads.  Water was reportedly over car doors in some areas, including the Milford train station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure passed to the south of the Tri-State Area. High precipitable water values caused very heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding in portions of Fairfield and New Haven counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Main St. and River Rd. were closed due to flooding in Oronoque, which is located within the township of Stratford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Beacon Falls, a sinkhole developed from flooding on Blackberry Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Irene made landfall locally as a tropical storm around 900 am August 28, 2011 over New York City.  Irene then moved across Southeast New York and Western Connecticut before dissipating over Northern New England near the Canadian Border the evening of August 28. The large envelope of winds associated with Tropical Storm Irene pushed a 3 to 5 foot surge of water into Long Island Sound resulting in moderate to major coastal flooding, wave damage and erosion.  This resulted in damage or destruction of over 100 homes in East Haven, Milford, Westport, Fairfield and Stratford.  Heavy damage to public beaches and other public and private facilities also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS tidal gauge at New London, CT recorded a maximum water level of 6.55 ft. MLLW at 924 am EST, above the unofficial NWS moderate coastal flooding benchmark.||These water levels cause moderate coastal flooding along the shore.  In Stonington, inundation of 1 to 3 feet over the seawall and inland about 100 feet into adjacent properties was experienced.  In Groton, Long Island Sound inundated portions of Beach Pond Rd., Tyler Avenue, Pine Island Rd., Jupiter Pt. Rd., Shore Ave and adjacent properties with 1 to 3 feet of water. Thames Street was inundated by 1 to 3 ft. of water, with the Thames Inn Boat House inundated by several feet of water. In addition, the Bayview Avenue Boat launch was inundated about 50 ft. inland.

This resulted in damage or destruction of over 100 homes in East Haven, Milford, Westport, Fairfield and Stratford.  Heavy damage to public beaches and other public and private facilities also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Old Saybrook, inundation of around 1 -2 feet over the seawall into the adjacent streets and properties in Saybrook Point was experienced. The Saybrook Point Marina was inundated by a few feet of water as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Irene made landfall locally as a tropical storm around 900 am August 28, 2011 over New York City.  Irene then moved across Southeast New York and Western Connecticut before dissipating over Northern New England near the Canadian Border the evening of August 28. The large envelope of winds associated with Tropical Storm Irene pushed a 3 to 5 foot surge of water into Long Island Sound resulting in moderate to major coastal flooding, wave damage and erosion.  This resulted in damage or destruction of over 100 homes in East Haven, Milford, Westport, Fairfield and Stratford.  Heavy damage to public beaches and other public and private facilities also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS tidal gauge at Bridgeport, CT recorded a maximum water level of 12.07 ft. MLLW at 1106 am EST, well above the NWS moderate coastal flooding benchmark of 10.4 feet. The Army Corps of Engineers tidal gauge at Stamford, CT recorded a maximum water level of 12.7 ft. MLLW at 1030 am EST, well above the NWS moderate coastal flooding benchmark of 11.1 feet.||These water levels caused widespread moderate to major inundation along the coast between 930 am and 1230 pm.  In Bridgeport, water from Long Island Sound came up through Bridgeport Harbor and flooded Bridgeport train station tracks. In Fairfield, the  combination of fresh water and surge inundated multiple sections of White Street with a few feet of water causing its closure. In addition, water inundation was experienced 1/2 mile inland on Fairfield Beach Rd past 1 Rod Highway. In Westport, the  combination of fresh water and surge inundated US1 with one feet of water as the Saugatuck River was forced out of its banks. Additionally closures were experienced along the Saugutuck River near Riverside and Imperial Ave. In Stamford, water covered about half of Cummings Park due to inundation from Long Island Sound.

Irene then moved across Southeast New York and Western Connecticut before dissipating over Northern New England near the Canadian Border the evening of August 28. The large envelope of winds associated with Tropical Storm Irene pushed a 3 to 5 foot surge of water into Long Island Sound resulting in moderate to major coastal flooding, wave damage and erosion.  This resulted in damage or destruction of over 100 homes in East Haven, Milford, Westport, Fairfield and Stratford.  Heavy damage to public beaches and other public and private facilities also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS tidal gauge at New Haven, recorded a maximum water level of 11.57 ft. MLLW at 1036 am on August 28 2011, which is well above the unofficial NWS moderate coastal flooding threshold for the area.||These tidal levels caused widespread moderate to major surge inundation along the coast.  In East Haven, several beach front homes suffered extensive damage from storm surge on Cosey Beach Ave. where 4 to 8 ft. of surge with battering wave action was estimated, causing structural damage to at least 2 dozen houses and swept at least 4 houses to sea.  Additionally, significant inundation was reported in the Short Beach section with a lot of damage on Little Bay Lane and Beckett Avenue and surrounding streets.  In West Haven, heavy damage was sustained to several coastal properties in Savin Rock.  In New Haven, inundation of several feet of water onto Longshore Drive from LI Sound make it impassable.  Additionally, 1 to 2 ft. of inundation occcurred across Long Wharf Drive and surrounding properties, including IKEA shopping center.  In Guilford, a combination of heavy rain and coastal flooding caused 2 to 3 feet of water to cover the roadway and properties surrounding the intersection of Water and River Streets along the West River.  In Branford, several feet of water inundated Linden Avenue and neighboring properties. This combined with wave action caused severe erosion and undermining of roadways in the area with about a dozen homes and businesses significantly damaged.  Along Seaview Avenue several houses were flooded and damaged with up to 6 feet of surge.  In Milford, businesses along Broadway near Naugatuck Avenue were inundated with 1 to 2 feet of water.  While along the immediate shoreline, the police reported sections of East Broadway inundated with up to 8 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure passed to the south of the Tri-State Area. High precipitable water values caused very heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding in portions of Fairfield and New Haven counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local authorities pumped the basements of several homes and businesses in Derby, Ansonia and near the intersection of Pearl and Day Streets in Seymour.  Flooding also occurred on Rt. 8 and Rt. 34 causing portions of the roads to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across the region during Wednesday afternoon and evening, June 1st. Atmospheric conditions were favorable for severe thunderstorms including supercells with an explosion environment in place across the region. The thunderstorms across the region during the afternoon hours had strong and persistent (sometimes rotating) updrafts. ||Severe thunderstorms occurred in Litchfield County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (measured) was reported during a thunderstorm in Burrville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area was in a warm, humid and very unstable air mass on Wednesday, June 8th. A strong disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere moved across the region tapping the explosive instability, triggering severe thunderstorms across Litchfield County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in Canaan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Bethlehem during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of low pressure riding along a nearly stationary frontal boundary across the local area caused several rounds of thunderstorms, which resulted in flash flooding in portions of Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of State St. and Iranistan Rd. in Bridgeport was closed due to three ft. of standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough and an approaching cold front caused a bout of widespread severe weather across most of Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported  down on South Meriden Street in Chesire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective System moved out of the Great Lakes and across New York state providing a focus for convection across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires along Harrisville Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere moved across the region Sunday afternoon and evening, August 21st. There were two distinct rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms. The first occurred during the late morning and early afternoon hours, and the second during the late afternoon into early evening. Mainly damaging winds occurred with the severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down in Sharon due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bermuda high pressure dominated the region with broad southwest flow aloft. This flow ushered in hot and humid air. Temperatures across Litchfield County warmed well into the 90s each day. On Friday July 22nd, the Thomaston Dam reported a high of 100 degrees.||A weak boundary marked by a shift in winds and lower dew points moved southward across the region on Friday, July 22nd. However, the boundary did not reach Litchfield County until late in day and this was too late to bring any relief to the oppressive heat and humidity. High dew points in the upper 60s to lower 70s, combined with the hot temperatures, resulted in heat indices of 105 to 110 degrees. The boundary dissipated over the area Friday evening. Saturday was another hot and humid day but not as oppressive as Friday was.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm approximately 2 miles southeast of Torrington.

Tropical Storm Irene tracked north northeast across eastern New York and western New England during Sunday, August 28th, producing widespread flooding, and damaging winds across the region. ||Irene tracked from a position over New York City around 8 AM EST Sunday, to approximately 10 miles west of Danbury, CT at 10 AM EST, to approximately 15 miles south of Pittsfield, MA at 1 PM EST Sunday, to approximately 65 miles south of Rutland, VT at 4 PM EST. ||The greatest impact from Irene across Litchfield County was due to heavy to extreme rainfall, which resulted in widespread flash flooding. Rainfall amounts generally averaged 5 to 10 inches. Much of the rain which fell occurred within a 12 hour period, beginning early Sunday morning, and ending Sunday evening. ||This heavy to extreme rainfall resulted in widespread flash flooding and river flooding across Litchfield County. Numerous road closures were reported due to flooding and downed trees and power lines, along with some evacuations. In addition, moderate flooding was reported on the Housatonic River at Falls Village and at Gaylordsville. The Gaylordsville gage was damaged by flood waters.||Strong winds also occurred across Litchfield County, with frequent wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph, along with locally stronger wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The strongest winds occurred from the north to northeast during the morning hours, then from the west to northwest during Sunday evening. The combination of strong winds, and extremely saturated soil led to numerous downed trees and power lines across the region. This also resulted in widespread long duration power outages. ||In particular, the approximate number of customers affected by power outages included: Litchfield County, 25000.||On September 2nd, 2011 a Major Disaster Declaration was declared, FEMA-4023-DR. This declaration made Individual Assistance and Public Assistance available to all 8 counties in the state of Connecticut. In addition, the declaration made Hazard Mitigation Grant Program assistance available. ||Individual Assistance: assistance to individuals and households. Public Assistance: assistance to state and local governments and certain private nonprofit organizations for emergency work and repair or replacement if disaster-damaged facilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported down by strong winds across Litchfield County, resulting in power outages and road closures including, but not limited to the following: in Harwinton, Wildcat Road, Clearview Avenue, Norton Passway and Locust Road; in Winsted, Old New Hartford Road; and in Falls Village at the intersection of Barns and Under Mountain Roads. In Torrington, an amateur radio person measured a wind gust of 43 mph at 8:32 am EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Irene tracked north northeast across eastern New York and western New England during Sunday, August 28th, producing widespread flooding, and damaging winds across the region. ||Irene tracked from a position over New York City around 8 AM EST Sunday, to approximately 10 miles west of Danbury, CT at 10 AM EST, to approximately 15 miles south of Pittsfield, MA at 1 PM EST Sunday, to approximately 65 miles south of Rutland, VT at 4 PM EST. ||The greatest impact from Irene across Litchfield County was due to heavy to extreme rainfall, which resulted in widespread flash flooding. Rainfall amounts generally averaged 5 to 10 inches. Much of the rain which fell occurred within a 12 hour period, beginning early Sunday morning, and ending Sunday evening. ||This heavy to extreme rainfall resulted in widespread flash flooding and river flooding across Litchfield County. Numerous road closures were reported due to flooding and downed trees and power lines, along with some evacuations. In addition, moderate flooding was reported on the Housatonic River at Falls Village and at Gaylordsville. The Gaylordsville gage was damaged by flood waters.||Strong winds also occurred across Litchfield County, with frequent wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph, along with locally stronger wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The strongest winds occurred from the north to northeast during the morning hours, then from the west to northwest during Sunday evening. The combination of strong winds, and extremely saturated soil led to numerous downed trees and power lines across the region. This also resulted in widespread long duration power outages. ||In particular, the approximate number of customers affected by power outages included: Litchfield County, 25000.||On September 2nd, 2011 a Major Disaster Declaration was declared, FEMA-4023-DR. This declaration made Individual Assistance and Public Assistance available to all 8 counties in the state of Connecticut. In addition, the declaration made Hazard Mitigation Grant Program assistance available. ||Individual Assistance: assistance to individuals and households. Public Assistance: assistance to state and local governments and certain private nonprofit organizations for emergency work and repair or replacement if disaster-damaged facilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 202 was reported closed due to flooding in New Milford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Housatonic River in Litchfield County. Flooding was reported but not limited to along Route 7 in Gaylordsville.||The Falls Village river gage located near Water Street in Falls Village exceeded its 7 foot flood stage at 12:23 pm EST August 28th, its 10 foot moderate flood stage at 6:08 pm, crested at 11.74 feet at 2 am August 29th, and fell below flood stage at 5:15 pm September 1st. ||The Gaylordsville river gage located near Route 7, Kent Road, less than a mile north of the Village of Gaylordsville exceeded its flood stage at 6:08 pm EST August 28th and fell below flood stage at 7:50 pm August 29th. The crest time and level are unknown since the stilling well gage failed. A pressure transducer gage was installed while the water was still rising.
